1:
"Now when Festus was come into the province, after three days he ascended from Caesarea to Jerusalem." The witnesses note the arrival of the new governor. The puritan Henry and the grammatical voices note that Festus, succeeding Felix (24:27), promptly went up to Jerusalem — the religious capital — within three days of entering his province, to acquaint himself with the affairs of the nation.

On the diligence, the witnesses note the new governor's eagerness to establish order. Festus, unlike the corrupt Felix, began his administration with energy, visiting Jerusalem early. Yet his diligence would be turned by the Jews to renew their attack on Paul.

The witnesses note the providence: Festus's visit to Jerusalem would occasion the Jews' renewed plot against Paul (vv.2-3), which would lead, through Paul's appeal, to his journey to Rome. The verse opens the account of Paul before Festus.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Festus, the new governor succeeding Felix (24:27), promptly went up from Caesarea to Jerusalem within three days — a diligent start to his administration (Henry), acquainting himself with the nation's affairs. The verse displays the new governor's energy: visiting the religious capital early. The verse marks the opening of the Festus narrative: the new procurator arriving, the Jews about to renew their attack on Paul (vv.2-3). The verse displays the providence in Festus's visit: his diligence would occasion the renewed plot, which through Paul's appeal would carry him to Rome. The verse displays the contrast with Felix: Festus began with energy and order, yet would prove no more just toward Paul. The arrival of the new governor marks a new stage in Paul's long custody: the case taken up afresh, the Jews seizing the opportunity to press their charges. The verse opens the chapter that would see Paul's appeal to Caesar — the pivotal act that secured his journey to Rome. The diligent new governor, visiting Jerusalem, sets in motion the events that would fulfill the Lord's promise that Paul must bear witness at Rome (23:11). The verse displays the providence working through the change of administration toward the apostle's appointed witness.
2:
"Then the high priest and the chief of the Jews informed him against Paul, and besought him," The witnesses note the Jews' renewed attack. The grammatical voices note the persistence of the hostility: two years after Paul's arrest, the high priest and Jewish leaders still pursued him, laying their charges before the new governor.

On "besought him," the witnesses note their urgency. They not only informed against Paul but earnestly entreated Festus — seeking a favor (v.3) that would deliver Paul into their hands. The leaders' relentless malice had not abated.

The witnesses note the corruption of the leadership: the high priest, who should uphold justice, conspiring against an innocent man. The verse displays the renewed persecution before the new governor.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the high priest and Jewish leaders, two years after Paul's arrest, renewed their attack — informing against him and earnestly entreating Festus, seeking a favor (v.3) to deliver Paul into their hands. The verse displays the persistence of the hostility: the leaders still pursuing Paul. The verse marks the renewed persecution before the new governor: the Jews seizing the opportunity of the change of administration. The verse displays the relentless malice of Paul's enemies: two years had not cooled their hatred. The verse displays the corruption of the leadership: the high priest conspiring against an innocent man. The renewed attack, the earnest entreaty, display the depth of the opposition Paul faced. The verse displays the providence amid the malice: the Jews' renewed plot would lead, through Paul's appeal, to his journey to Rome. The verse marks the Jews seizing the new governor's inexperience, hoping to turn his desire for popularity to their advantage. The leaders' persistent pursuit of Paul displays both their malice and the providence that would use it to advance the gospel. The verse sets up the specific request (v.3): to bring Paul to Jerusalem, where the assassins lay in wait. The renewed persecution, the earnest entreaty, mark the resumption of the conflict that would issue in Paul's appeal and his witness at Rome. The verse displays how implacable religious hatred can outlast every delay: two years had passed, a governor had fallen, and still the leaders pressed their suit as if no time had intervened. Their eagerness to entreat a stranger-governor on the very heels of his arrival betrays how much they had staked on Paul's destruction.
3:
"And desired favour against him, that he would send for him to Jerusalem, laying wait in the way to kill him." The witnesses note the renewed plot to assassinate Paul. The grammatical voices note the revived conspiracy. As two years before (23:12-15), the Jews planned an ambush — asking Festus to bring Paul to Jerusalem, intending to kill him on the way.

On the treachery, the witnesses note the abuse of the legal request. The leaders sought a "favor" (the same word used of the earlier plot) — to have Paul transferred, so the assassins could strike. The same murderous scheme was revived.

The witnesses note the persistence of the malice: the same plot, the same treachery, two years later. The verse displays the renewed conspiracy against Paul's life.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Jews revived their plot — asking Festus to bring Paul to Jerusalem, intending to ambush and kill him on the way (as 23:12-15) — seeking a "favor" that masked murder. The verse displays the renewed conspiracy: the same treacherous scheme, two years later. The verse marks the persistence of the malice: the assassination plot revived against the new governor. The verse displays the depth of the hatred: the leaders, unable to condemn Paul legally, again resorting to ambush. The verse displays the treachery: the legal request cloaking the murderous intent. The verse displays God's providence amid the renewed danger: Festus's refusal (v.4) would foil the plot, as the captain's action had foiled the earlier one. The verse displays the relentless determination of Paul's enemies: the same plot, the same treachery, undeterred by two years' delay. The revived conspiracy, seeking Paul's transfer to enable his murder, displays the malice that pursued the apostle. Yet the providence that had preserved Paul before would preserve him again: Festus, for his own reasons, would keep Paul at Caesarea (v.4). The verse marks the renewed threat to Paul's life and the providence that would deliver him, carrying him toward the appeal and the witness at Rome. The verse displays the moral distance between the accusers and their pretended cause: men who claimed to defend the law were willing to abandon it entirely, asking a Roman governor to become the unwitting accomplice of a roadside murder. The same plot, revived after two years, shows a hatred that neither time nor failure could cool.
4:
"But Festus answered, that Paul should be kept at Caesarea, and that he himself would depart shortly thither." The witnesses note Festus's refusal of the request. The grammatical voices note the providential refusal. Festus, for his own reasons (perhaps protocol, perhaps suspicion), declined to bring Paul to Jerusalem, keeping him at Caesarea where his court was held.

On the providence, the witnesses note God's hand in the refusal. Whether or not Festus knew of the plot, his decision to keep Paul at Caesarea foiled the assassination, as the captain's action had before. God used the governor's procedure to preserve Paul.

The witnesses note Festus's intention to return shortly to Caesarea, inviting the accusers there. The verse displays the providential refusal that preserved Paul.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Festus refused to bring Paul to Jerusalem, keeping him at Caesarea (where his court was held) and inviting the accusers there — a providential refusal that foiled the assassination plot, whether or not Festus knew of it. The verse displays the providential refusal: Paul kept safe at Caesarea. The verse marks the foiling of the plot: Festus's procedure preserving Paul. The verse displays God's hand in the governor's decision: the assassination thwarted by Festus keeping Paul at Caesarea. The verse displays the providence working through ordinary administration: Festus, for his own reasons, declining the request that would have delivered Paul to the assassins. The verse displays the contrast with the Jews' hope: they sought Paul's transfer; Festus kept him safe. The refusal, however motivated, preserved Paul as the captain's earlier action had. The verse displays the consistency of God's providence: repeatedly delivering Paul from the murderous plots through the Roman authorities. The verse marks the second foiling of an assassination plot: Festus's refusal, like Lysias's escort, preserving the apostle. The providential decision to keep Paul at Caesarea displays God's care, frustrating the conspirators and carrying Paul toward the appeal and the witness at Rome. The verse displays the divine protection working through the governor's procedure, the plot defeated, the apostle preserved. The verse displays how God's protection often wears the plain dress of bureaucratic routine. Festus had no thought of shielding Paul; he simply preferred to keep the case at his own seat of judgment. Yet that ordinary preference, no more than a procedural convenience, became the wall behind which the apostle was kept safe from forty waiting blades.
5:
"Let them therefore, said he, which among you are able, go down with me, and accuse this man, if there be any wickedness in him." The witnesses note Festus's proper procedure. The grammatical voices note the fairness of the proposal. Festus invited the accusers to come to Caesarea and bring their charges — the orderly Roman process, requiring the accusers to substantiate their case before the court.

On "if there be any wickedness," the witnesses note the conditional. Festus, not yet knowing the facts, invited a proper trial. The Reformed Calvin notes the governor's apparent fairness, though it would prove hollow when he later sought to please the Jews (v.9).

The witnesses note the contrast with the Jews' plot: they sought ambush; Festus offered a lawful trial. The verse displays the proper procedure of the Roman court.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Festus invited the able men among the Jews to come to Caesarea and accuse Paul, if there were any wickedness in him — the orderly Roman process requiring the accusers to substantiate their charges before the court (Calvin noting the apparent fairness, later proven hollow, v.9). The verse displays the proper procedure: a lawful trial at Caesarea, not an ambush at Jerusalem. The verse marks Festus's reasonable proposal: the accusers to bring their case to the proper court. The verse displays the contrast with the Jews' treachery: they sought murder; Festus offered justice (at least in form). The verse displays the Roman insistence on proper procedure: the accusers and the accused before the tribunal, the charges to be proven. The verse displays the providence in the orderly process: Paul to be tried at Caesarea, beyond the reach of the assassins. The proposal, fair in appearance, would expose the emptiness of the Jews' charges (vv.7-8). The verse displays the standard of Roman justice — accusers confronting the accused, charges substantiated — that providentially protected Paul. Yet Festus's fairness was shallow, as his later willingness to please the Jews would show (v.9). The verse marks the convening of the proper trial that would reveal the baselessness of the accusations and lead to Paul's appeal. The orderly procedure, however hollow in Festus, served God's providence in preserving and advancing the apostle.
6:
"And when he had tarried among them more than ten days, he went down unto Caesarea; and the next day sitting on the judgment seat commanded Paul to be brought." The witnesses note Festus's prompt return and the convening of the trial. The grammatical voices note the governor's diligence. After his stay at Jerusalem, Festus returned to Caesarea and, the very next day, took the judgment seat to hear Paul's case.

On "the judgment seat," the witnesses note the formal tribunal (the bema). Festus sat in official judgment, commanding Paul brought before him — the proper legal setting for the trial.

The witnesses note the providence: the trial convened, the charges to be aired, leading to Paul's appeal. The verse displays the formal convening of the case.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: after some ten days at Jerusalem, Festus returned to Caesarea and the next day took the judgment seat (the bema), commanding Paul brought — the prompt, formal convening of the trial. The verse displays the diligent governor: returning and immediately hearing the case. The verse marks the formal trial: Festus on the tribunal, Paul brought before him. The verse displays the proper legal setting: the judgment seat, the accused summoned, the case to be heard. The verse displays the providence in the prompt trial: the charges to be aired, the baselessness exposed, leading to Paul's appeal. The verse displays Festus's energy: not delaying like Felix, but convening the trial at once. The formal setting — the judgment seat, the summons — displays the orderly Roman process. The verse marks the resumption of Paul's case before the new governor: the trial that would reveal the emptiness of the charges (vv.7-8) and issue in the appeal to Caesar (vv.10-11). The verse displays the providence carrying Paul forward through the legal process toward Rome. The prompt convening of the trial, the formal tribunal, set the stage for the confrontation between Paul and his accusers that would lead to the pivotal appeal. The verse displays the diligent governor and the orderly process that providentially advanced Paul's case toward its appointed end at Rome.
7:
"And when he was come, the Jews which came down from Jerusalem stood round about, and laid many and grievous complaints against Paul, which they could not prove." The witnesses note the Jews' many unprovable charges. The grammatical voices note the contrast between the gravity of the charges and the absence of proof. Barnes and others note the Jews surrounded Paul, hurling many serious accusations, yet able to prove none.

On "grievous complaints," the witnesses note their character. As before (24:5-6), the charges included sedition, profaning the temple, and heresy — grave accusations, but without evidence. The Jews' malice exceeded their proof.

The Reformed Calvin notes the providence: the very inability to prove the charges vindicated Paul, exposing the malice of his accusers. The verse displays the baseless accusations.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Jews from Jerusalem surrounded Paul, hurling many grievous charges (sedition, profaning the temple, heresy, as 24:5-6) — yet could prove none. The verse displays the contrast: grave accusations without evidence, the malice exceeding the proof. The verse marks the emptiness of the charges: many and serious, but unprovable. The verse displays the vindication of Paul: the very failure of proof exposed the falsehood of the accusations (Calvin). The verse displays the malice of the accusers: heaping up charges they could not substantiate. The verse displays the providence in the failure of proof: Paul vindicated by the accusers' inability to make their case. The baseless accusations, many and grievous but unprovable, display the injustice of the persecution. The verse displays the standard of evidence the Jews could not meet: surrounding Paul with charges, but producing no proof. The verse marks the collapse of the prosecution: the accusations exposed as empty. The very gravity of the unproven charges displays the desperation of Paul's enemies, resorting to slander when they had no evidence. The verse displays God's providence using the trial to vindicate Paul: the accusers' failure proving his innocence, as every Roman magistrate would confirm (v.18-19, 25). The baseless complaints set up Paul's confident defense (v.8) and the appeal that would follow. The verse displays the self-defeating nature of malice without truth: the more numerous and grievous the charges, the more glaring their want of proof. A just cause needs few words and solid evidence; an unjust one heaps up accusations to hide its emptiness, and so the very volume of the complaints became a testimony to Paul's innocence.
8:
"While he answered for himself, Neither against the law of the Jews, neither against the temple, nor yet against Caesar, have I offended any thing at all." The witnesses note Paul's threefold denial. The grammatical voices note the comprehensive defense. Paul denied any offense against the three things charged: the Jewish law, the temple, and Caesar. His denial covered both the religious and the political accusations.

On "against Caesar," the witnesses note the political charge. The Jews had accused Paul of sedition (treason against Rome); Paul firmly denies any offense against Caesar, asserting his loyalty as a Roman citizen.

The witnesses note the confidence of innocence. Paul's clear, threefold denial displays his clear conscience and the baselessness of the charges. The verse displays his comprehensive defense.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul denied any offense against the Jewish law, the temple, or Caesar — a comprehensive defense covering both the religious and political charges, firmly asserting his innocence and loyalty. The verse displays Paul's threefold denial: clear, confident, comprehensive. The verse marks the apostle's defense: rebutting every charge, religious and political. The verse displays the confidence of innocence: Paul's clear conscience answering the baseless accusations. The verse displays the breadth of the defense: against the law (the religious charge), the temple (the charge of defilement), and Caesar (the charge of sedition) — Paul offended in none. The verse displays the apostle's loyalty: no offense against Caesar, no sedition against Rome. The comprehensive denial, covering every accusation, displays both Paul's innocence and the emptiness of the charges. The verse displays the contrast between the Jews' many unprovable complaints (v.7) and Paul's clear, confident denial. The verse marks the apostle's vindication of himself before Festus: innocent of all charges, religious and political. Paul's threefold denial sets up the crisis (v.9): Festus, knowing Paul innocent yet wishing to please the Jews, would propose a trial at Jerusalem, provoking the appeal to Caesar. The verse displays the apostle's clear conscience and comprehensive defense against the baseless accusations of his enemies. The verse displays the calm symmetry of a clear conscience: against three distinct heads of accusation Paul returns three flat denials, neither evading nor elaborating. His brevity is itself a kind of evidence, for the innocent need not multiply explanations; he simply meets each charge with the truth and leaves it there before the judge.
9:
"But Festus, willing to do the Jews a pleasure, answered Paul, and said, Wilt thou go up to Jerusalem, and there be judged of these things before me?" The witnesses note Festus's compromise — and condemn his corruption of justice. On "willing to do the Jews a pleasure," the grammatical voices note the politics. Barnes: "Desirous of securing their favour... In this he evinced rather a desire for popularity than an inclination to do justice. Had he been disposed to do right at once, he would have immediately discharged Paul." Robertson: "Festus, like Felix, falls a victim to fear of the Jews."

On the proposal, the witnesses note its danger. Robertson: Festus, "while wanting Paul to think that he would in Jerusalem 'be judged of these things before me,' in reality probably intended to turn Paul over to the Sanhedrin." The proposal would have exposed Paul to the assassins.

The Reformed Calvin notes the swift corruption: "we see how soon those are drawn unto all corruption which are not guided by the Spirit of God... ambition, and peradventure desire of gain, got the upper hand." Wesley notes the providence: God overruled it to give Paul occasion to appeal to Rome.

The witnesses agree: Festus, wishing to please the Jews (desiring popularity over justice, Barnes; falling to fear of the Jews like Felix, Robertson), proposed that Paul be tried at Jerusalem — a dangerous proposal that would have exposed Paul to the Sanhedrin and the assassins (Robertson). Calvin condemns the swift corruption: ambition and gain overcoming justice. Wesley notes the providence: God overruled it to occasion Paul's appeal to Rome. The verse displays the corruption of the judge: Festus, knowing Paul innocent (v.18-19), yet proposing to gratify the Jews. The verse marks the crisis that provoked the appeal: the unjust proposal threatening Paul's life. The verse displays the failure of Roman justice in Festus: like Felix, sacrificing justice for popularity. The verse displays the danger to Paul: a trial at Jerusalem, where his enemies waited. The verse displays the providence overruling the corruption: Festus's unjust proposal would drive Paul to appeal to Caesar, fulfilling the promise of Rome (23:11). The corruption of the judge, condemned by the witnesses, became the occasion of God's purpose: the appeal that would carry Paul to Rome. The verse displays both the injustice of Festus and the providence that used it for the gospel's advance.
10:
"Then said Paul, I stand at Caesar's judgment seat, where I ought to be judged: to the Jews have I done no wrong, as thou very well knowest." The witnesses note Paul's firm assertion of his rights. On "Caesar's judgment seat," the grammatical voices note Paul's claim as a Roman citizen. Barnes: "I regarded the tribunal before which I then stood... as really the judgment-seat of Caesar... I have a right to a fair and impartial trial." Paul insists on Roman justice, not transfer to the Jewish tribunal.

On "where I ought to be judged," the witnesses note the necessity. Robertson: "Where I must be judged, for δε expresses necessity." As a Roman citizen, Paul had the right to be tried before the Roman court, not handed to the Jews.

On "as thou very well knowest," the witnesses note the sharp rebuke. Robertson: Paul "exposes the conduct of Festus with merciless precision" — Festus knew Paul had done no wrong (confirmed by his own confession, v.18-19), yet sought to gratify the Jews. The verse displays Paul's bold defense of his rights.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul firmly asserted his right as a Roman citizen — "I stand at Caesar's judgment seat, where I ought to be judged" — refusing transfer to the Jewish tribunal (Barnes), insisting on Roman justice (the necessity, Robertson). The "as thou very well knowest" sharply rebukes Festus, who knew Paul innocent (v.18-19) yet sought to please the Jews (Robertson: exposing Festus with merciless precision). The verse displays Paul's bold defense of his rights: claiming the protection of Roman law. The verse marks the apostle's firm stand: refusing the unjust proposal, asserting his citizenship. The verse displays the boldness of innocence: Paul, conscious of no wrong, demanding justice. The verse displays the rebuke of Festus: the governor knowing Paul innocent yet proposing to gratify his enemies. Paul's assertion — at Caesar's judgment seat, where he ought to be judged — displays his rightful claim to Roman justice. The verse displays the apostle's clear conscience: "to the Jews have I done no wrong." The bold defense, the sharp rebuke, the assertion of citizenship display Paul's firm stand against the corruption of the judge. The verse sets up the appeal (v.11): Paul, denied justice by Festus, would carry his cause to Caesar himself. The verse displays the apostle's lawful insistence on his rights and his exposure of the governor's injustice.
11:
"For if I be an offender, or have committed any thing worthy of death, I refuse not to die: but if there be none of these things whereof these accuse me, no man may deliver me unto them. I appeal unto Caesar." The witnesses note Paul's submission to just law and his appeal to Caesar. On "I refuse not to die," the grammatical voices note Paul's noble submission. Barnes: "I have no wish to escape justice... No true Christian wishes to escape from the laws." Paul submits to just punishment if guilty, displaying his respect for law.

On "no man may deliver me unto them," the witnesses note the legal force. Robertson: "Paul is a Roman citizen and not even Festus can make a free gift of Paul to the Sanhedrin." No one could lawfully hand him to his enemies.

On "I appeal unto Caesar," the witnesses note the right and the providence. Robertson and Barnes note the Roman citizen's right of appeal to the emperor, carrying the case to Rome. The Reformed Calvin notes the lawful use of the magistrate: Paul "freely useth such helps as he could find at the hands of men... God, who hath appointed judgment-seats, doth also grant liberty to his to use the same lawfully." Barnes notes the providence: God answering Paul's long-cherished prayer to see Rome (Romans 1:10) in an unexpected way.

The witnesses agree: Paul submitted to just law ("if I be an offender... I refuse not to die" — no Christian wishes to escape justice, Barnes) yet asserted that no one could lawfully deliver him to the Jews (a Roman citizen, Robertson), and appealed to Caesar — the citizen's right of appeal to the emperor (Robertson, Barnes), carrying the case to Rome. Calvin notes the lawful use of the magistrate's help: God grants liberty to use the courts. Barnes notes the providence: God answering Paul's prayer to see Rome (Romans 1:10) in an unexpected way. The verse displays Paul's noble submission and his momentous appeal. The verse marks the pivotal act: the appeal to Caesar that would carry Paul to Rome. The verse displays both Paul's respect for law (submitting if guilty) and his rightful use of his citizenship (appealing when denied justice). The appeal, far from evading justice, sought it before the highest court. The verse displays the providence in the appeal: the means by which God fulfilled his promise (23:11) and Paul's desire to see Rome. The verse displays the lawful use of civil rights in the cause of God: Paul, denied justice by Festus, carrying his cause to Caesar. The momentous appeal, submitting to just law yet claiming the citizen's right, marks the turning-point that would bring the apostle to Rome.
12:
"Then Festus, when he had conferred with the council, answered, Hast thou appealed unto Caesar? unto Caesar shalt thou go." The witnesses note Festus's granting of the appeal. On the council, the grammatical voices note the governor's advisers. Robertson: the συμβουλιον was "the chief officers and personal retinue of the procurator, his assessors." Festus consulted them on whether to grant the appeal.

On the granting, the witnesses note Festus's relief. Barnes: "He was willing in this way to rid himself of this trial... He did not dare to deliver him to the Jews... and was not willing to do justice to Paul... He was therefore probably rejoiced at the opportunity." The appeal freed Festus from his dilemma.

On the tone, the witnesses note a possible sneer. Ellicott: "There is obviously something like a sneer in the procurator's acceptance... He knew better than the Apostle to what kind of judge the latter was appealing." Festus, perhaps with indignation or scorn, granted the appeal. The verse displays the granting that sealed Paul's journey to Rome.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Festus, having conferred with his council (his assessors, Robertson), granted the appeal — "unto Caesar shalt thou go" — relieved to be rid of the troublesome case (Barnes), perhaps with a sneer (knowing what kind of judge Nero was, Ellicott). The verse displays the granting of the appeal: Paul's journey to Rome now certain. The verse marks the sealing of the appeal: Festus consenting, Paul bound for Caesar. The verse displays the governor's relief: the appeal extricating him from his dilemma (unable to please the Jews without injustice, unwilling to free Paul). The verse displays the providence in the granting: the appeal accepted, the journey to Rome assured, fulfilling the Lord's promise (23:11). The verse displays the irony: Festus, glad to be rid of Paul, unwittingly advancing God's purpose. The possible sneer — "unto Caesar shalt thou go" — displays the governor's attitude, yet served the divine plan. The verse marks the decisive moment: the appeal granted, Paul's course set for Rome. The granting of the appeal, however motivated, displays God's providence carrying the apostle toward the witness at Rome he was promised. The verse displays the convergence of human politics and divine purpose: Festus's relief and God's plan both served by the granting of the appeal that would bring Paul to Caesar.
13:
"And after certain days king Agrippa and Bernice came unto Caesarea to salute Festus." The witnesses note the arrival of Agrippa and Bernice. The grammatical voices note their identity. Agrippa is Herod Agrippa II, the last of the Herodian kings; Bernice his sister. The witnesses note (with the historians) the scandal of their relationship — Bernice living with her brother in a manner that occasioned suspicion of incest.

On the visit, the witnesses note it was a courtesy call. The new king came to salute (welcome) the new governor — a diplomatic visit between the Roman procurator and the Jewish client-king.

The puritan Henry and others note the providence: the visit would occasion Paul's appearance before Agrippa (vv.22-23), and the noblest of his defenses (ch. 26). The verse introduces the royal visitors.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Agrippa (Herod Agrippa II, the last Herodian king) and Bernice his sister (their relationship scandalous, occasioning suspicion of incest) came to Caesarea to salute the new governor — a diplomatic courtesy call. The verse displays the arrival of the royal visitors: the Jewish client-king welcoming the Roman procurator. The verse marks the providential occasion: Agrippa's visit would lead to Paul's appearance before him (vv.22-23) and his great defense (ch. 26). The verse displays the historical setting: the Herodian dynasty's last representative, the Roman administration, the intersection of Jewish and Roman power. The verse displays the providence in the visit: a routine diplomatic call would become the occasion for Paul to bear witness before a king (as foretold, 9:15). The verse introduces the figures before whom Paul would make his noblest defense. The scandal of Agrippa and Bernice, noted by the witnesses, displays the moral corruption of the Herodian house even as it providentially set the stage for the gospel's proclamation. The verse marks the gathering at Caesarea that would issue in the grand audience (v.23) and Paul's testimony before Agrippa. The arrival of the royal visitors, a courtesy call, displays the providence preparing the occasion for the apostle to bear witness before kings, fulfilling his commission to carry Christ's name before the Gentiles and their rulers.
14:
"And when they had been there many days, Festus declared Paul's cause unto the king, saying, There is a certain man left in bonds by Felix:" The witnesses note Festus laying Paul's case before Agrippa. The grammatical voices note the governor's purpose: perplexed about what to write to Caesar (v.26), Festus sought Agrippa's help, the king being knowledgeable in Jewish matters.

On "left in bonds by Felix," the witnesses note Felix's injustice. Felix had left Paul a prisoner to please the Jews (24:27); Festus inherited the case. The mention displays the long, unjust custody.

The witnesses note Festus's candor: laying the whole matter before Agrippa, seeking counsel. The verse displays the governor consulting the king about Paul's case.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: after several days, Festus laid Paul's case before Agrippa — seeking the king's help (Agrippa knowledgeable in Jewish matters) with his perplexity about what to write to Caesar (v.26) — noting Paul had been left in bonds by Felix (24:27, displaying the long unjust custody). The verse displays Festus consulting Agrippa: laying the case before the Jewish king. The verse marks the governor's perplexity: needing counsel on a case he could not understand. The verse displays the inheritance of injustice: Paul left a prisoner by Felix, now Festus's burden. The verse displays Festus's candor in seeking Agrippa's help: a Roman governor consulting a Jewish king on a Jewish religious matter. The verse displays the providence in the consultation: Festus laying the case before Agrippa would lead to Paul's appearance and testimony before the king. The mention of Felix's leaving Paul in bonds displays the protracted, unjust custody the apostle had endured. The verse marks the beginning of Festus's account to Agrippa (vv.14-21), which would expose the baselessness of the charges and the heart of the matter — the resurrection of Jesus (v.19). The verse displays the governor's reliance on the king's knowledge and the providence preparing Paul's defense before Agrippa. Festus's consultation, seeking help with his predicament, advances the narrative toward the grand audience and the apostle's great testimony. The verse displays the strange providence by which a pagan governor's embarrassment became the occasion of the gospel's advance. Festus, out of his depth in a matter of Jewish religion, turns to Agrippa for help; and in that turning, unknowingly, he sets in motion the hearing at which Paul would proclaim Christ before a king.
15:
"About whom, when I was at Jerusalem, the chief priests and the elders of the Jews informed me, desiring to have judgment against him." The brief verse continues Festus's account. The grammatical voices note the report of the Jews' demand. Festus recounts how the chief priests and elders, at Jerusalem, laid charges against Paul and sought a verdict of condemnation.

On "desiring to have judgment against him," the witnesses note the demand for condemnation. The Jews sought not a fair trial but a verdict against Paul — a condemnation, even a sentence of death. Their demand revealed their prejudice.

The witnesses note Festus's recounting of the pressure he faced. The verse displays the Jews' demand for Paul's condemnation, as reported to Agrippa.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: Festus recounts how the chief priests and elders at Jerusalem informed against Paul, demanding a verdict of condemnation — seeking not justice but Paul's condemnation (even death). The verse displays the Jews' demand: not a fair trial but a sentence against Paul. The verse marks Festus's account of the pressure: the leaders pressing for condemnation. The verse displays the prejudice of the accusers: demanding a verdict against Paul before any proof. The verse displays the injustice the Jews sought: a condemnation, not a trial. The verse displays Festus's recounting to Agrippa of the demand he faced at Jerusalem. The demand for "judgment against him" — a verdict of guilt — displays the malice and prejudice of Paul's enemies, who wanted not justice but his destruction. The verse displays the contrast with Roman procedure (v.16): the Jews demanding condemnation, the Romans requiring a proper trial. The verse marks the continuation of Festus's account, exposing the Jews' demand for Paul's condemnation. The leaders' desire for a verdict against Paul, without trial or proof, displays the persecution he endured and the injustice the Roman process (however flawed in Festus) providentially restrained. The verse advances Festus's narrative toward the heart of the matter — the resurrection (v.19) — and the providence that would bring Paul before Agrippa to testify. The verse displays the difference between accusation and justice: the leaders did not ask Festus to try Paul but to condemn him, seeking a verdict already settled in their own hearts. Their demand for judgment against the man, before any evidence was weighed, exposes a tribunal of prejudice masquerading as a court of law.
16:
"To whom I answered, It is not the manner of the Romans to deliver any man to die, before that he which is accused have the accusers face to face, and have licence to answer for himself concerning the crime laid against him." The witnesses note Festus's appeal to Roman justice. The grammatical voices note the principle. Festus cites the Roman rule: no man condemned without confronting his accusers and having opportunity to defend himself — the principle of a fair trial.

On the Roman principle, the witnesses note its justice. Clarke draws the lesson: "condemn no man unheard, and confront the accusers with the accused... a sacred maxim with every magistrate." The Roman insistence on this principle providentially protected Paul.

The witnesses note the contrast with the Jews' demand: they sought condemnation without trial; Rome required the accused to face his accusers and defend himself. The verse displays the Roman principle of justice.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Festus cites the Roman principle — no man condemned without confronting his accusers and having opportunity to defend himself (Clarke: condemn no man unheard, a sacred maxim) — refusing the Jews' demand for condemnation without trial. The verse displays the Roman principle of justice: the accused to face his accusers and answer the charge. The verse marks the contrast: the Jews seeking condemnation, Rome requiring a fair trial. The verse displays the justice of the Roman rule: the accused given opportunity to defend himself, the accusers required to be present. The verse displays the providence in the Roman principle: it protected Paul from the Jews' demand for summary condemnation. Clarke's lesson stands: this maxim — condemn no man unheard — should govern all magistrates and Christians. The verse displays the standard of justice that providentially shielded Paul: the right to confront accusers and answer charges. The verse displays Festus's account of his proper procedure, refusing the unjust demand. The Roman principle, however imperfectly applied by Felix and Festus, providentially preserved Paul from the murderous prejudice of his enemies. The verse marks the appeal to the standard of fair trial — a principle that protected the apostle and reflects the justice God requires. The verse displays the providence using the Roman legal tradition to shield Paul and advance him toward Rome.
17:
"Therefore, when they were come hither, without any delay on the morrow I sat on the judgment seat, and commanded the man to be brought forth." The brief verse continues Festus's account of his prompt procedure. The grammatical voices note the governor's diligence: without delay, the next day, he convened the trial (as v.6). Festus emphasizes his promptness in handling the case.

The witnesses note Festus presenting himself to Agrippa as a diligent, fair judge. He recounts his immediate convening of the court, contrasting (implicitly) with the Jews' demand for summary condemnation.

The verse displays Festus's account of his prompt trial: the judgment seat taken, Paul brought, the case heard without delay. The verse continues the governor's narrative to Agrippa.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: Festus recounts his prompt procedure — without delay, the next day, he took the judgment seat and commanded Paul brought (as v.6) — presenting himself to Agrippa as a diligent, fair judge. The verse displays Festus's account of his promptness: the trial convened at once. The verse marks the governor's self-presentation: emphasizing his diligence and fairness. The verse displays the contrast (implicit) with the Jews' demand for summary condemnation: Festus convening a proper trial without delay. The verse displays the orderly procedure: the judgment seat, the summons, the hearing. The verse displays Festus recounting his handling of the case to Agrippa, stressing his promptness and fairness. The verse, brief as it is, continues the governor's narrative, leading toward the heart of the matter — the charges and their baselessness (v.18), and the resurrection of Jesus (v.19). The verse displays Festus's concern to appear a just and diligent judge before the king. The prompt convening of the trial, recounted here, displays the Roman procedure that providentially protected Paul. The verse advances Festus's account toward the revelation that the charges concerned not crimes but religious questions — chiefly the resurrection — which the governor could not comprehend. The verse marks the continuation of the narrative that would lead to Agrippa's wish to hear Paul (v.22) and the grand audience. The verse displays the contrast Festus is careful to draw for the king: where the Jews wanted summary condemnation, he, a Roman, would not so much as delay the trial, but took his seat at once and heard the man. The promptness he claims is meant to commend his own justice, and it incidentally underscores how baseless the charges proved to be.
18:
"Against whom when the accusers stood up, they brought none accusation of such things as I supposed:" The witnesses note Festus's surprise at the charges. The grammatical voices note his expectation. Barnes: "It was natural that Festus should suppose that they would accuse Paul of some such offence" — a breach of the peace, a violation of Roman law, some atrocious crime. The two years' custody and the Jews' violence suggested grave guilt.

On the actual charges, the witnesses note the anticlimax. Clarke: "how surprised was he to find that scarcely any thing that amounted to a crime was laid to his charge." The accusers brought no real crime, only religious disputes.

The Reformed Calvin notes the providence: the very emptiness of the charges vindicated Paul. The verse displays Festus's surprise at the baselessness of the accusations.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Festus expected grave charges (a breach of the peace, a crime against Rome, given the long custody and the Jews' violence, Barnes) but was surprised that the accusers brought no real crime (Clarke: scarcely anything amounting to a crime). Calvin notes the providence: the emptiness of the charges vindicated Paul. The verse displays Festus's surprise: the anticlimax of baseless accusations. The verse marks the exposure of the charges' emptiness: the governor expecting crime, finding only religious dispute. The verse displays the vindication of Paul: even his judge confessing the charges baseless. The verse displays the contrast between the Jews' violent persecution and the absence of any real crime: their malice exceeding any wrong Paul had done. The verse displays the providence in Festus's confession: the governor's own surprise testifying to Paul's innocence. The verse marks Festus's candid account to Agrippa: the charges were not what he supposed, but mere religious questions. The governor's surprise, recounted to the king, displays the baselessness of the accusations and the injustice of Paul's long custody. The verse sets up the heart of the matter (v.19): not a crime, but a dispute about the resurrection of Jesus. The verse displays the providence using even the pagan governor's testimony to vindicate the apostle, exposing the emptiness of the charges his enemies had pressed for years.
19:
"But had certain questions against him of their own superstition, and of one Jesus, which was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be alive." The witnesses note Festus's pagan-eye view of the gospel — the resurrection reduced to a "question." On "superstition," the grammatical voices note the neutral term. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note δεισιδαιμονία is used neutrally here (Festus would not insult the Jewish king Agrippa); it means "religion," the national worship.

On "one Jesus, which was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be alive," the witnesses note the heart of the gospel from a contemptuous view. Robertson: "the climax of supercilious scorn toward both Paul and 'one Jesus'... With all his top-loftical airs Festus has here correctly stated the central point of Paul's preaching about Jesus as no longer dead, but living." Barnes: "In this manner a Roman magistrate could speak of the glorious truth of the Christian religion."

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley laments Festus's coldness: "How coldly does he mention the things of the last importance!... why, O Festus, didst thou doubt concerning it? Only because thou didst not search into the evidence." The verse displays the worldling's dismissal of the resurrection.

The witnesses agree: Festus described the dispute as concerning "their own superstition" (the neutral term for religion, not to insult Agrippa, Barnes, Robertson) and "one Jesus, which was dead, whom Paul affirmed to be alive" — the heart of the gospel reduced to a contemptuous "question" (Robertson: supercilious scorn, yet correctly stating the central point of Paul's preaching — Jesus alive, not dead). Barnes notes how a Roman magistrate could thus dismiss the glorious truth; Wesley laments Festus's coldness, doubting because he would not search the evidence. The verse displays the worldling's dismissal of the resurrection: the cardinal truth treated as a trivial dispute. The verse marks the pagan-eye view of the gospel: Festus reducing the resurrection of Christ to "one Jesus... affirmed to be alive." The verse displays the irony: Festus, with all his scorn, correctly states the heart of Paul's message — that Jesus lives. The verse displays the tragedy of unbelief: the most important truth treated with indifference because unexamined (Wesley). The verse displays the centrality of the resurrection: even the dismissive governor identifies it as the core of Paul's preaching. The verse displays both the world's contempt for the gospel and the unmistakable centrality of the risen Christ — the resurrection, scorned by Festus, the very heart of the apostolic message.
20:
"And because I doubted of such manner of questions, I asked him whether he would go to Jerusalem, and there be judged of these matters." The witnesses note Festus's perplexity and his proposal. On "doubted," the grammatical voices note the perplexity. Robertson: "being perplexed... to be in doubt which way to turn." Ellicott: "his perplexity is his justification for bringing the matter before a prince who, being a Jew, might be a better judge."

On the proposal to go to Jerusalem, the witnesses note Festus's account (vv.9). He frames his proposal as motivated by perplexity about the religious questions — though Luke earlier noted his desire to please the Jews (v.9). Both motives may have operated (Robertson).

The witnesses note Festus presenting his conduct favorably to Agrippa, attributing his proposal to perplexity rather than politics. The verse displays the governor's account of his proposal.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Festus, perplexed about the religious questions (in doubt which way to turn, Robertson), proposed that Paul be tried at Jerusalem — framing his proposal as motivated by perplexity, though Luke earlier noted his desire to please the Jews (v.9), both motives perhaps operating (Robertson). Ellicott notes his perplexity justified consulting Agrippa, a Jew. The verse displays the governor's perplexity and his account of his proposal. The verse marks Festus presenting his conduct favorably: attributing the proposal to perplexity, not politics. The verse displays the governor's genuine bewilderment: unable to comprehend the religious dispute, especially the resurrection. The verse displays the contrast between Festus's account (perplexity) and Luke's earlier note (desire to please the Jews, v.9): the governor putting his conduct in a favorable light. The verse displays the perplexity of the pagan judge before the gospel: unable to grasp the questions, seeking help. The verse marks Festus's recounting of his proposal to Agrippa, leading toward the king's wish to hear Paul (v.22). The governor's perplexity, genuine in part, displays the inability of the worldly mind to comprehend the things of God. The verse displays the providence in Festus's bewilderment: his need for help would bring Paul before Agrippa to testify. The perplexity of the judge, recounted to the king, advances the narrative toward the grand audience and the apostle's great defense.
21:
"But when Paul had appealed to be reserved unto the hearing of Augustus, I commanded him to be kept till I might send him to Caesar." The witnesses note Festus recounting Paul's appeal. On "Augustus," the grammatical voices note the imperial title. Robertson and Ellicott note Σεβαστός (Augustus, the Revered) was the title assumed by Octavius and his successors, here applied to Nero — the reigning emperor.

On "the hearing," the witnesses note the legal examination. Robertson: διάγνωσις, "the regular word for a legal examination, thorough sifting." Paul's appeal carried his case to the emperor's tribunal.

The witnesses note Festus's account of granting the appeal: Paul to be kept until sent to Caesar. The verse displays the governor recounting the appeal that necessitated the present consultation. The verse marks the appeal as the reason for Festus's predicament (vv.26-27).

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Festus recounts that Paul appealed to be kept for the hearing of Augustus (the imperial title, here Nero, Robertson, Ellicott) — a legal examination before the emperor (διάγνωσις, Robertson) — so Festus kept him to send to Caesar. The verse displays the governor recounting the appeal: Paul's case carried to the emperor. The verse marks the appeal as the reason for the consultation: Festus must send Paul, but has nothing certain to write (vv.26-27). The verse displays the imperial title: Augustus, the Revered, applied to the emperor, reflecting the emperor-worship the Christians opposed. The verse displays the legal process: the appeal to the highest court, the case bound for Rome. The verse displays Festus's predicament emerging: having granted the appeal, he must send Paul, yet has no clear charge to report. The verse marks the recounting of the appeal that set Paul's course for Rome and created Festus's dilemma. The verse displays the providence in the appeal: Paul reserved for the emperor's hearing, the journey to Rome assured. The governor's account of the appeal, leading to his need for help in framing charges, advances the narrative toward Agrippa's wish to hear Paul (v.22) and the grand audience. The verse displays the appeal as the pivot: Paul bound for Caesar, Festus seeking help, the providence carrying the apostle toward Rome.
22:
"Then Agrippa said unto Festus, I would also hear the man myself. To morrow, said he, thou shalt hear him." The witnesses note Agrippa's wish to hear Paul. On the wish, the grammatical voices note its motivation. Barnes: "Agrippa doubtless had heard much of the fame of Jesus, and of the new sect of Christians; and probably he was induced by mere curiosity." Clarke compares Herod's curiosity to see Jesus (Luke 23:8).

On the providence, the witnesses note the great occasion. Barnes: "This wish of Agrippa gave occasion to the noblest defence which was ever made before any tribunal, and to as splendid eloquence as can be found anywhere in any language" (ch. 26). Paul would bear witness before a king.

The Reformed Calvin notes the cold curiosity yet the providential fruit: even if none believed sincerely, the testimony confirmed the godly and shamed the enemies. The verse displays Agrippa's wish and the providential occasion.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Agrippa wished to hear Paul — induced by curiosity (having heard of Jesus and the Christians, Barnes; like Herod's curiosity to see Jesus, Luke 23:8, Clarke) — and Festus promised he should hear him the next day. Barnes notes this gave occasion to Paul's noblest defense (ch. 26); Calvin notes the cold curiosity yet the providential fruit (confirming the godly, shaming the enemies). The verse displays Agrippa's wish and the providential occasion: Paul to testify before a king. The verse marks the setting up of the great defense: Agrippa's curiosity becoming the occasion for the apostle's witness. The verse displays the providence in the king's wish: a request born of curiosity becoming the means of the gospel's proclamation before kings (as foretold, 9:15). The verse displays the contrast between Agrippa's cold curiosity and the momentous testimony it would occasion. The verse displays the divine purpose using human curiosity: Agrippa wished to hear Paul, and thereby provided the occasion for one of the greatest defenses of the faith. The verse marks the arrangement of the grand audience (v.23) and Paul's testimony before Agrippa. Agrippa's wish, however motivated, displays the providence preparing the occasion for the apostle to bear witness before a king, fulfilling his commission to carry Christ's name before the Gentiles and their rulers. The verse displays God's providence turning curiosity into the occasion of the gospel's proclamation.
23:
"And on the morrow, when Agrippa was come, and Bernice, with great pomp, and was entered into the place of hearing, with the chief captains, and principal men of the city, at Festus' commandment Paul was brought forth." The witnesses note the grand audience and its pomp. The grammatical voices note the splendor. Agrippa and Bernice came "with great pomp" (phantasia, display) — royal robes, retinue, the chief captains and principal men of Caesarea gathered for the occasion.

Chrysostom and the witnesses note the contrast: the worldly pomp of the king and the chained apostle. The great display of earthly grandeur set against the humble prisoner who possessed the truth.

The witnesses note the providence: the great assembly — kings, captains, principal men — gathered to hear Paul, a splendid audience for the gospel (as Festus intended, vv.26). The verse displays the grand audience before which Paul would testify.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Agrippa and Bernice came with great pomp (royal display, retinue), entering the hall with the chief captains and principal men of Caesarea, and Paul was brought forth — a grand audience for the apostle's testimony. The witnesses note the contrast (Chrysostom): the worldly pomp set against the chained apostle who possessed the truth. The verse displays the splendid assembly: kings, captains, notables gathered to hear Paul. The verse marks the grand audience: the stage set for Paul's great defense (ch. 26). The verse displays the contrast between earthly grandeur and the humble prisoner: the pomp of Agrippa and Bernice against the bound apostle. The verse displays the providence in the splendid audience: the gospel to be proclaimed before kings and notables, fulfilling Paul's commission (9:15). The verse displays the irony: the great pomp of the worldly powers gathered to hear a prisoner who possessed what they lacked — the truth of Christ. The grand audience, with all its display, providentially provided the platform for the apostle's witness. The verse marks the assembling of the splendid company before which Paul would make his noblest defense. The contrast between the worldly pomp and the chained apostle displays the difference between earthly grandeur and spiritual riches. The verse displays God's providence using the vanity of the great to gather an audience for the gospel — kings and captains brought together to hear the testimony of the risen Christ from his bound apostle.
24:
"And Festus said, King Agrippa, and all men which are here present with us, ye see this man, about whom all the multitude of the Jews have dealt with me, both at Jerusalem, and also here, crying that he ought not to live any longer." The witnesses note Festus's introduction of Paul's case. The grammatical voices note the governor's summary: the whole multitude of the Jews, at Jerusalem and Caesarea, had pressed for Paul's death, crying he ought not to live.

On the Jews' demand, the witnesses note its vehemence. The leaders and people alike had demanded Paul's death — a clamor for his life, displaying the intensity of the persecution.

The witnesses note Festus presenting the case to the assembly, setting up his predicament (vv.26-27). The verse displays the governor's introduction of Paul before the grand audience.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Festus introduced Paul's case to Agrippa and the assembly — the whole multitude of the Jews, at Jerusalem and Caesarea, had demanded Paul's death, crying he ought not to live. The verse displays the governor's summary: the intense, widespread demand for Paul's death. The verse marks Festus's introduction of the case to the grand audience. The verse displays the vehemence of the persecution: the whole multitude clamoring for Paul's life. The verse displays the contrast: the violent demand of the Jews against the absence of any real crime (v.18). The verse displays Festus presenting his predicament: a prisoner whose death the Jews demanded, yet who had committed no crime worthy of death (v.25). The verse displays the clamor for Paul's death, the persistent and widespread hostility. The verse marks the setting up of Festus's dilemma: how to handle a prisoner whom the Jews wanted dead but who was guilty of nothing. The intensity of the Jews' demand, recounted to the assembly, displays the persecution Paul endured and the injustice of the clamor against him. The verse displays the providence in the public hearing: the case laid before kings and notables, the gospel about to be proclaimed. The verse advances Festus's account toward his confession of Paul's innocence (v.25) and his predicament about what to write (vv.26-27), and toward Paul's great defense before the assembled dignitaries.
25:
"But when I found that he had committed nothing worthy of death, and that he himself hath appealed to Augustus, I have determined to send him." The witnesses note Festus's confession of Paul's innocence and his decision. On "nothing worthy of death," the grammatical voices note the verdict. Like every Roman magistrate (Gallio, Lysias, Felix), Festus found Paul guilty of no capital crime. The governor's own confession vindicates the apostle.

On the appeal, the witnesses note the necessity it created. Having appealed to Augustus, Paul must be sent to Rome — the appeal binding Festus to forward the case to the emperor.

The witnesses note the irony: Festus confessing Paul innocent, yet sending him to Caesar (because of the appeal Paul made to escape the governor's injustice). The verse displays the confession of innocence and the decision to send Paul.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Festus confessed he found Paul guilty of nothing worthy of death (like every Roman magistrate — Gallio, Lysias, Felix) and, since Paul had appealed to Augustus, determined to send him to Rome. The verse displays the confession of innocence: the governor's own verdict vindicating Paul. The verse marks the decision to send Paul: the appeal necessitating the journey to Caesar. The verse displays the consistent Roman testimony: Paul guilty of no crime, confessed by his own judge. The verse displays the irony: Festus confessing Paul innocent, yet sending him to Caesar — because Paul had appealed to escape the governor's injustice (v.9-11). The verse displays the providence in the decision: Paul, innocent yet appealing, bound for Rome to fulfill the Lord's promise (23:11). The confession of innocence, joined to the decision to send Paul, displays both the vindication of the apostle and the providence carrying him to Rome. The verse displays the predicament emerging: an innocent prisoner bound for the emperor, with no clear charge to report (vv.26-27). The verse marks the governor's candid admission of Paul's innocence and the necessity of the appeal. The confession that Paul had done nothing worthy of death, from the very judge sending him to Caesar, displays the injustice of his custody and the providence using the appeal to advance him toward the witness at Rome.
26:
"Of whom I have no certain thing to write unto my lord. Wherefore I have brought him forth before you, and specially before thee, O king Agrippa, that, after examination had, I might have somewhat to write." The witnesses note Festus's predicament and his purpose. On "no certain thing to write," the grammatical voices note the absurdity. Barnes: Festus was "about to send a prisoner to Rome to be tried, who had appealed from his jurisdiction; and yet he was ignorant of the charges against him." He had no clear crime to report.

On "my lord," the witnesses note the imperial title. Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott note that "lord" (Dominus, Kyrios) had been refused by Augustus and Tiberius as "accursed and ill-omened," but was accepted by Caligula and Nero — reflecting the growing emperor-worship the Christians opposed.

On the purpose, the witnesses note Festus's hope. He brought Paul before Agrippa to gain, through the king's examination, something definite to write to Caesar. The verse displays the governor's predicament and his reliance on Agrippa.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Festus confessed he had no certain charge to write to "my lord" (the imperial title, refused by Augustus and Tiberius but accepted by Nero, reflecting emperor-worship, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott) — the absurdity of sending a prisoner without a charge (Barnes) — and brought Paul before Agrippa hoping the king's examination would yield something to write. The verse displays the governor's predicament: an innocent prisoner bound for Caesar, no crime to report. The verse marks Festus's reliance on Agrippa: needing the Jewish king's help to frame charges. The verse displays the absurdity of the situation: a prisoner sent to the emperor with no accusation. The verse displays the title "my lord," the emperor-worship the gospel confronted: the Christians could not call Caesar "Lord," that title belonging to Christ alone. The verse displays the providence in the predicament: Festus's need bringing Paul before Agrippa to testify, the gospel proclaimed before a king. The verse marks the governor's candid confession of his dilemma and his hope that Agrippa's examination would resolve it. The predicament — no certain thing to write — displays the injustice of Paul's custody (an innocent man bound) and the providence using Festus's difficulty to occasion Paul's great defense. The verse displays God's providence turning the governor's perplexity into the platform for the apostle's witness before kings.
27:
"For it seemeth to me unreasonable to send a prisoner, and not withal to signify the crimes laid against him." The witnesses note Festus's reasonable admission — and the lessons of the chapter. On the absurdity, the grammatical voices note the governor's predicament. Barnes: "it would be proper that the charges should be all specified... Yet Festus could do neither." Sending a prisoner without stated charges was unreasonable.

On the lessons, Barnes draws three: Agrippa's curiosity (hearing yet unmoved); Festus's worldly indifference (treating Christianity as a question that did not concern him — "strange that it did not occur to Festus that if [Jesus] was alive, his religion was true"); and Paul's earnest devotion (staking all on the truth, ready to defend it before any tribunal).

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke draws the lesson: "From St. Paul's appeal to Caesar, we see that it is lawful to avail ourselves, even in the cause of God, of those civil privileges with which his mercy has blessed us." The verse displays Festus's reasonable admission and the providence.

The thin comment matches the verse, which closes the chapter. The witnesses agree: Festus admitted it unreasonable to send a prisoner without specifying the charges (Barnes) — hence the hearing before Agrippa to find something to write. Barnes draws the chapter's lessons: Agrippa's curiosity (hearing yet unmoved), Festus's worldly indifference (treating the gospel as no concern of his, never asking whether Jesus's being alive made his religion true), and Paul's earnest devotion (staking all on the truth). Clarke notes the lawful use of civil privileges in God's cause (Paul's appeal). The verse displays Festus's reasonable admission and his predicament: a prisoner to send, no charge to report. The verse marks the close of the chapter: the governor's dilemma occasioning Paul's defense (ch. 26). The verse displays the absurdity that providentially served the gospel: Festus's need bringing Paul before the king. The verse displays the contrasting responses to the gospel: Agrippa's curiosity, Festus's indifference, Paul's devotion. The verse displays the lawful use of civil rights: Paul's appeal, advancing God's purpose. The verse displays the providence in the governor's predicament: the difficulty of framing charges occasioning the grand audience and the apostle's testimony. The verse marks the transition to Paul's great defense before Agrippa (ch. 26), set up by Festus's need for help. The reasonable admission — unreasonable to send a prisoner without charges — displays the injustice of Paul's custody and the providence using the governor's dilemma to give the apostle a platform before kings. The verse displays God's providence turning Festus's perplexity into the occasion of the gospel's proclamation before the assembled dignitaries.
